findings.claim: mode_elements carry ONLY {head, geo} — NO position (x_m) or extent — so D5's per-mode range-gating / close-efficacy 'how far out each mode's working element sits' has no data to read. The composite-weapon per-mode selection cannot know a mode's reach.
findings.evidence: In weapons.py every mode_elements entry is `dict(head=..., geometry=...)` (e.g. poleaxe lines 176-180, guisarme 396-399, bec_de_corbin 432-436, kama_yari 220-224, voulge 379-382). Verified at runtime: for poleaxe/guisarme/bec_de_corbin/kama_yari/voulge the baked mode_element keys are exactly ['geo','head'] — no x_m, no extent_m. systems._mode_elements(w) (line 244) and element_afforded(el,w) (line 259) consume only el['geo'] and el['head']. The plan's D5 says each mode's close-efficacy is 'derived from its OWN geo' — that part is satisfiable — but the plan's own §D4/D5 framing ('how far out each mode's working element sits') and the driving symptom ('composites with real point-elements never trigger deflection'; approach_displace gates on static w['head'] not the selected mode) require the mode's forward position. That position lives ONLY in the parallel mass `elements` list, and mode_elements is NOT linked to elements by any key — they are matched purely by list ORDER and comment text, and their lengths need not agree (flamberge has 3 elements / 0 mode_elements; poleaxe 3/3; a consumer cannot safely zip them). D5 as written would need to (a) add x_m/extent to each mode_element, or (b) author an explicit elements<->mode_elements index. Neither exists.
findings.recommendation: Before I4 (mode/measure coupling), extend each mode_element to carry the working element's x_m and extent_m (or an explicit `element_ref` index into the mass `elements` list). Populate from the Phase-0 head_elements[].x_m already collected (present in phase0_morphology_combined.json for every composite). Add a CI/import assertion that every mode_element resolves to a real located element so the two parallel views cannot silently drift. Until this exists, D5's per-mode reach term is fabricated, not derived.

findings.claim: D9 hook_affordance cannot distinguish a genuine HOOK (pull/trap/unhorse: guisarme hook, guandao rear notch, ji crescent, poleaxe/bec beak) from a straight forward-catching LUG/WING/TINE (ranseur wing-lug, spetum prong, partisan wing-lug, dangpa flank-tine) — the CURRENT schema collapses all of them to one boolean `dual_role_element=True` with no hook-vs-catch geometry. The discriminating fields were collected in Phase 0 and dropped.
findings.evidence: In weapons.py every catching feature is a guard with `dual_role_element=True` and (redundantly) the same extent_m as its element, carrying no orientation, no `type`, no hook/pull semantics: guisarme hook (line 391), guandao rear notch (319), ji crescent (408), spetum prongs (274-275), partisan wing-lugs (288-289), dangpa flank-tines (234-235), kama_yari cross-blades (215-216), voulge rear_fluke (375), fauchard back_hook (343), hook_sword crescent (725). The Phase-0 artifact phase0_morphology_combined.json DID capture the distinction: a per-guard `type` field ('wing' for ranseur/partisan lugs) and rich geom_notes ('often slightly hooked for a catch/pull function' for the bec beak; 'catching/hooking an opponent's blade' for dangpa tines with the explicit note the center prong is elongated to PREVENT lodging; 'active trap-and-bind catching surface rather than a slashing edge' for the partisan) — plus `orient_deg` (see separate finding). weapons.py drops `type`, geom_notes, and orient_deg entirely. A grep confirms NO hook_affordance() or hook-geometry field exists anywhere in the engine. So D9 as scoped ('grab affinity derives from ... hook hardware (dual_role_element guards + hook_affordance(w) saturating sum)') can only sum `dual_role_element` extent — which treats a spetum's forward bind-lug identically to a guisarme's pull-hook. Note also Flag 6 in phase0_report.md: hook_sword's crescent is double-listed under both head_elements and guards with shared mass — 'the single item most likely to bite a downstream consumer that naively sums both lists', and D9's saturating sum over guards is exactly such a consumer.
findings.recommendation: D9 must invent at least one new per-guard schema field (a hook/pull flag or a re-mapped orient_deg sign) OR restore the Phase-0 `type` + orient_deg; the claim that D9 needs 'no data edits' is only true for retiring the `clinch` scalar, not for building hook_affordance from morphology. Recommend re-ingesting orient_deg (rear-curved beak = negative orient => pull-capable) and `type` from phase0_morphology_combined.json as the physical discriminator, and adding a CI check that hook_affordance never reads the `clinch` scalar it is meant to replace. Confirm the hook_sword crescent is counted once (element only), matching _all_parts' existing dual_role exclusion at weapon_physics.py:126.

findings.claim: Phase 0 collected `orient_deg` (fore/aft/perpendicular orientation) and per-part `material` on EVERY element and guard, and the final weapons.py schema dropped BOTH. orient_deg is precisely the fore/aft primitive that D5 (arc-swing vs in-line thrust close-efficacy) and D6/D9 (rear-facing beak, weapon facing) implicitly assume, and it is gone.
findings.evidence: grep for `orient_deg` or `material` in weapons.py returns 0 matches; grep in phase0_morphology_combined.json returns them on every element/guard (~124 orient_deg occurrences). The values are load-bearing, not cosmetic: bec_de_corbin hammer face orient_deg=+90 (perpendicular strike), curved beak orient_deg=-90 (REAR-facing), top spike orient_deg=0 (in-line thrust) — phase0 lines 2096/2109/2122; lucerne_hammer identical pattern (+90/-90/0, lines 2203/2216/2229). In weapons.py these three elements are all flattened to positive x_m (0.5478/0.587/0.8217 for bec) with the fore/aft distinction erased — the engine cannot tell an in-line thrust spike from a rear-curved hooking beak. This is exactly the geometry D5 needs to make 'a broad arc-requiring swing collapse in tight quarters while a thrust barely degrades' emerge per-mode rather than by fiat, and what a physically-grounded (non-class) facing term (D6) would key on. phase0_report.md §2 asserts 'No AXIS violations' but never flags that the orientation axis was intentionally dropped — it simply never made the schema.
findings.recommendation: Re-ingest orient_deg onto elements/mode_elements (0=in-line thrust axis, ±90=lateral/perpendicular, sign=fore/aft) so D5's close-efficacy and D9's hook-vs-thrust discrimination derive from a real primitive instead of a [FIAT] geo guess. Material is lower-priority (mass is already specimen-apportioned) but note its absence means any future edge-durability/bind-shear term would also have to re-source it. Flag in the plan's 'Fable 5 audit findings (folded in)' block — it already anticipates 'Phase 0 collected orient_deg + per-part material that weapons.py dropped'; this finding confirms and scopes it.

findings.claim: D1/D7 rear_clearance ('physical length trailing behind the working hand') is derivable from the located-part x_m positions, BUT the ready-made length field that derive() exposes — length_m = (head_len+grip_len)*UNIT_M — is the WRONG quantity and is a tempting shortcut that would give badly wrong rear-clearance for exactly the composites and half-swords the plan calls out.
findings.evidence: weapon_physics.derive() returns length_m = Lh+Lg (line 160), the full weapon length, not the trailing extent behind the working hand (x=0). Computed per-weapon, length_m diverges sharply from the true rear extent (min over _all_parts of x-extent/2): spear length_m=2.01 but true rear extent -0.36m (butt); guisarme length_m=2.10 vs rear extent -1.06m; poleaxe 1.80 vs -0.90; estoc_halfsword 1.57 vs -1.17; longsword_halfsword 1.20 vs -0.78; naginata 2.10 vs -1.02. The CORRECT rear_clearance data DOES exist — haft.x_m/extent_m, butt.x_m, pommel.x_m are all present and _all_parts() already assembles them about the x=0 working-hand axis — so the primitive is sufficient. The hazard is that rear_clearance() will be tempted to read the convenient derive()['length_m'] and be wrong. The two auto-switch half-sword forms carry a correctly SHIFTED-origin part list (phase0_report.md Flag 5: origin_shift_m=0.75), so their rear extent is right ONLY if computed from parts, not from length_m.
findings.recommendation: Compute rear_clearance strictly as -min(x - extent/2 over _all_parts(w)) (i.e. how far the most-rearward part trails behind x=0), NOT from length_m. Add a unit test asserting rear_clearance(spear) < rear_clearance(guisarme) and that both half-sword forms exceed their base form (choking up trails more weapon). Do not add a new stored length field; derive from the existing parts.

findings.claim: D2's at_circumstance(w, grip, range_avail) grip-position argument is well-bounded by the EXISTING schema — grip validation is NOT a schema gap. (Recorded as an explicit non-blocker to close the audit question.)
findings.evidence: weapon_physics.grip_choke_max(w) (line 448), grip_travel_max(w) (444), and _gather_len(w) (436) already derive per-weapon grip bounds from grip_len (+head_len for hafted_tip), and at_grip(w,g) (454) clamps g to [0,1] and the forward slide u to [0, min(PoB, grip_travel_max)] — 'never past the inertia minimum'. Verified: mace/dagger/longsword grip_choke_max=0.0 (cannot gather), spear/staff/poleaxe=1.0, longsword_halfsword=0.8. So a nonsensical grip (beyond the haft, inside the head) is impossible by construction; at_circumstance can safely alias at_grip. The one caveat: bounds are keyed off grip_len/head_len only, not off the actual located haft.extent_m, so a weapon whose grip_len disagrees with its haft extent_m would be validated against the length primitive rather than the part geometry — a consistency check worth adding but not a sufficiency blocker.
findings.recommendation: Reuse grip_choke_max/grip_travel_max as the at_circumstance grip clamp (no new schema needed). Optionally add an import-time assertion that each weapon's grip_len*UNIT_M is consistent with its haft/grip part extent_m to catch future drift.

findings.claim: D6 (facing must never be per-weapon-class) is not directly tempted by any facing-proxy field in the current schema — the categorical `head` token is a damage-mode token, not a facing token — but the ABSENCE of orient_deg (see SERIOUS finding) means a physically-grounded facing term has no per-element geometry to key on and will remain a pure Combatant-state fiat as the plan intends. Low risk of a class shortcut, but the neutral-default facing cannot later be made physical without the dropped orient data.
findings.evidence: weapons.py `head` ∈ {point,cut_thrust,straight_cut,curved_cut,blunt} is consumed only as a damage/mode discriminator (systems.legibility line 431-437, approach_displace line 448, select_mode). Nothing in the schema encodes a weapon's presentation angle or bilateral profile. The plan's D6 writes facing as per-beat Combatant state via facing_target(c,closed,cfg) 'keyed on stance/measure/grip — NEVER weapon class', which the schema neither blocks nor assists. The only per-weapon geometry that a legitimate profile/facing term would want (element lateral orientation) is the dropped orient_deg.
findings.recommendation: Keep facing as Combatant state per the plan; do NOT introduce a per-weapon facing field. If the reach_sigma/legibility 'small profile term' (D6) is ever meant to be weapon-shaped, it must draw on re-ingested orient_deg, not on `head`. No action needed unless the profile term is promoted from neutral.
